metadata.summary: Long audio processing (>25MB), speaker diarization, and production TranscriptionService with provider routing (OpenAI, faster-whisper, local Whisper).

content.title: Speech-to-Text Advanced
content.summary: Long audio processing (>25MB), speaker diarization, and production TranscriptionService with provider routing (OpenAI, faster-whisper, local Whisper). Covers chunked transcription via LongAudioTranscriber, speaker labeling via SpeakerDiarizer (pyannote 3.1), and known bugs that must be patched before production use.

why.p: Whisper API rejects files above 25MB and returns no speaker attribution. Chunking with overlap prevents boundary-word loss; diarization adds "who said what" labels required for meeting and interview pipelines. Without pre-caching the pyannote model (~1.5GB) the first diarization request fails mid-run in production.

list.item: Audio files larger than 25MB need transcription (Whisper API hard limit)
Multi-speaker recordings (meetings, podcasts, interviews) need speaker attribution
Long-form transcription (1+ hour files) requiring timestamps and segment structure
Building a production STT service with provider fallback (cloud vs. local GPU)
Audio under 25MB and single speaker — use speech-to-text-basics direct API call; LongAudioTranscriber adds unnecessary overhead
Real-time (live) transcription — Whisper is batch-only; use Deepgram or AssemblyAI streaming
GPU unavailable and diarization needed — pyannote on CPU is ~10x real-time; impractical
Language is non-standard or heavily accented without prior quality validation

section.name: Long Audio Chunking
section.p: LongAudioTranscriber pattern for files exceeding the 25MB Whisper API limit, with overlap strategy and known bug fix.

rule.statement: Check file size in MB, not duration, before selecting transcription strategy. A 1-hour 64kbps mp3 is ~28MB and will fail the 25MB API limit even though it is less than an hour.
rule.rationale: The Whisper API limit is applied to the compressed audio file size, not to duration. A medium-quality mp3 can exceed 25MB well within a single hour of audio.

rule.statement: Return response.text from _transcribe_chunk, not the raw response object. The merge step expects strings.
rule.rationale: The original template returns the full response object. _merge_results then concatenates objects instead of strings, silently producing garbage output.

example.title: Correct _transcribe_chunk implementation

rule.statement: Set overlap_ms to 2000-5000ms when chunking audio. After merging, run deduplication on the chunk boundary to remove repeated words.
rule.rationale: Without overlap, words at chunk boundaries are cut off and lost. With overlap, the same words appear twice at every boundary — both halves are wrong without dedup.

rule.statement: Load audio chunks from disk one at a time, not all at once. A 2-hour WAV at 16kHz 16-bit is ~230MB in RAM; loading all chunks into memory will OOM in constrained environments.
rule.rationale: pydub loads audio as full PCM in memory. Chunking on disk — writing each chunk to a temp file and discarding after transcription — keeps memory footprint constant.

example.title: Memory-efficient chunk processing

rule.statement: Preprocess audio to 16kHz mono WAV before transcription to reduce file size and match Whisper's training format.
rule.rationale: Whisper was trained on 16kHz mono audio. Stereo 44kHz files are twice the size for no quality benefit; preprocessing prevents unnecessary file-size limit violations.

section.name: Speaker Diarization
section.p: SpeakerDiarizer using pyannote 3.1: HuggingFace token setup, GPU requirement, alignment with transcript segments, and production gotchas.

rule.statement: Pre-cache the pyannote speaker-diarization-3.1 model (~1.5GB) during container build, not on the first transcription request.
rule.rationale: Downloading 1.5GB mid-request causes request timeouts in production. The model must be present before any diarization call runs.

rule.statement: Do not use pyannote diarization on CPU for production workloads. CPU inference is ~10x real-time, making a 1-hour file take 10 hours to process.
rule.rationale: pyannote requires GPU (CUDA) for acceptable performance. Without it, diarization is functionally unusable for anything beyond short clips.

example.title: Proper pyannote initialization with GPU detection

rule.statement: Run diarization separately from transcription; align by timestamp midpoint. Do not interleave diarization into the transcription loop.
rule.rationale: Interleaving creates coupling between the two models, complicates error handling, and prevents independent retries of either step.

rule.statement: Add a boundary check when using segment midpoint for alignment: if (start + end) / 2 falls outside the speaker segment due to float rounding for sub-100ms segments, fall back to UNKNOWN rather than misassigning.
rule.rationale: Very short segments (speech artifacts) can produce midpoints that fall outside their own boundaries after floating-point arithmetic. Silent misassignment corrupts speaker labels for nearby segments.

rule.statement: Pass the actual number of speakers in the audio to pyannote (num_speakers parameter) rather than relying on auto-detection. Auto-detection over-segments mixed recordings.
rule.rationale: pyannote auto-detection creates spurious speaker IDs for background noise, laughter, or breathing in close-mic recordings. Known speaker count produces cleaner separation.

section.name: Production TranscriptionService
section.p: TranscriptionService provider routing rules, strategy selection logic, caching, and critical agent gotchas.

rule.statement: Route transcription requests by file size: under 5MB → direct API call; 5-25MB → standard TranscriptionService; over 25MB → LongAudioTranscriber chunking.
rule.rationale: Direct API calls avoid service initialization overhead for small files. Chunking is required above 25MB (Whisper API limit); standard service handles the middle range.

example.title: Provider routing by file size

rule.statement: Cache transcription results keyed on a SHA-256 of the audio file bytes (not the filename). Re-transcribing identical audio wastes money.
rule.rationale: Filenames are reused across pipeline runs. Content hashing ensures the cache key is stable even when files are re-uploaded with the same name.

rule.statement: TranscriptionService._transcribe_local delegates to FasterWhisperTranscriber and LocalWhisper which are not defined in the base template — implement these before using the LOCAL and FASTER_WHISPER providers.
rule.rationale: The template references these classes but does not define them. Calling the service with these providers will raise NameError at runtime without custom implementations.

rule.statement: word_timestamps=True requires response_format="verbose_json". The template sets this correctly, but response.words uses getattr with None default — validate that word timestamps are present before using them downstream.
rule.rationale: getattr with None default means missing word timestamps fail silently. Downstream subtitle or search-index generation will break without an explicit presence check.

rule.statement: The default supported_formats list does not include .ogg. Add it or convert .ogg to .mp3 before calling TranscriptionService.
rule.rationale: .ogg is common in web recording APIs (MediaRecorder default). Without explicit support, the service raises ValueError and the audio is lost.

section.name: Agentic Workflow
section.p: An orchestrator agent receives an audio file path and determines processing strategy: if file is less than 25MB and single-speaker, call OpenAI Whisper directly; if large, route to LongAudioTranscriber; if multi-speaker, add SpeakerDiarizer after transcription. Results are assembled into a structured transcript with timestamps and optional speaker labels. A review agent validates transcript completeness (non-empty, duration matches) before passing downstream.

list.item: haiku — Execute transcription: load file, select strategy, call TranscriptionService.transcribe(), return structured result
sonnet — Diarization + alignment: call SpeakerDiarizer.align_with_transcript(), format speaker-labeled output
sonnet — Transcript quality check: scan for repeated segments (chunking artifact), flag unusually short transcripts relative to audio duration

section.code:
<task> Transcribe audio file. Path: {{AUDIO_PATH}} File size: {{SIZE_MB}}MB Speakers: {{NUM_SPEAKERS}} (1 = single, 2+ = diarization needed) Language: {{LANGUAGE_CODE}} (null = auto-detect) Word timestamps: {{BOOL}}
Strategy: - If size > 25MB: use LongAudioTranscriber - If speakers > 1: add SpeakerDiarizer after transcription - Provider: openai (fallback: faster_whisper if local GPU available)
Return: {"text": "...", "segments": [...], "speakers": [...] or null} </task>

list.item: Preprocess audio to 16kHz mono WAV before transcription — reduces file size and matches Whisper's training format
For diarization: run pyannote separately from transcription, then align by timestamp midpoint; do not interleave diarization into transcription loop
Use faster-whisper locally for development and high-volume batch; use OpenAI API for production when GPU is unavailable
Store word-level timestamps if downstream use requires searchable transcripts or subtitle generation
For meetings: cap num_speakers at actual participant count in pyannote for better separation
Validate chunk overlap logic: overlapping chunks will produce duplicate content; implement text deduplication on merge
Cache transcription results by audio file hash (not filename) — re-transcribing identical audio costs real money
Use language parameter when language is known — auto-detect adds ~1s latency and occasionally misidentifies short clips
Whisper API 25MB limit applies to the compressed audio file, not the duration — a 1-hour 64kbps mp3 is ~28MB and will fail; convert to lower bitrate or chunk before calling
pyannote model download on first use (~1.5GB) can fail mid-request in production — pre-cache during container build, not at request time

SpeakerDiarizer.align_with_transcript uses segment_mid = (start + end) / 2 — if a speaker segment is very short (less than 100ms), the midpoint may fall outside the speaker segment due to float rounding; add boundary check
Whisper output is not punctuated on some audio types — downstream agents processing raw transcript text must not assume sentence structure

TranscriptionService raises ValueError on unsupported format, but supported_formats defaults to [".mp3", ".wav", ".m4a", ".flac", ".webm", ".mp4"] — .ogg is a common format not in the list; agents must convert before calling
Large audio files loaded via pydub hold full PCM in memory — a 2-hour WAV at 16kHz 16-bit is ~230MB in RAM; chunk on disk, not in memory
Groq Whisper uses the same API format as OpenAI but has stricter file size limits (check current limits); test before routing production traffic

template.purpose: TranscriptionService, LongAudioTranscriber, SpeakerDiarizer with bug fixes applied
Agent task prompt for transcription subagent with strategy routing

section.name: Services and Tools
section.p: CLI tools for local development and deployment:

list.item: openai (Python SDK) — Whisper API (whisper-1); install with pip install openai
faster-whisper — Local Whisper with CTranslate2 (faster); install with pip install faster-whisper
pyannote-audio — Speaker diarization; install with pip install pyannote.audio
pydub — Audio chunking and format conversion; install with pip install pydub
torch — GPU inference for pyannote; install with pip install torch
ffmpeg — Audio preprocessing (mono, resample to 16kHz); install with apt install ffmpeg
OpenAI Whisper API (SaaS) — Yes, Python SDK available. 25MB limit; word timestamps; $0.006/min; no real-time
Deepgram (SaaS) — Yes, REST API. Real-time streaming; speaker diarization; best for live audio
AssemblyAI (SaaS) — Yes, Python SDK. Speaker diarization; async long audio; PII redaction
Groq Whisper (SaaS) — Yes, OpenAI-compatible API. 10x faster than OpenAI Whisper; same model; cheap
faster-whisper (OSS) — Yes, local Python. CTranslate2 backend; 4x faster than openai-whisper on GPU
openai-whisper (OSS) — Yes, local Python. Reference implementation; slowest; most portable
pyannote-audio 3.1 (OSS) — Yes, local Python. Best open diarization; requires GPU for reasonable speed
WhisperX (OSS) — Yes, local Python. Whisper + diarization combined; word-level timestamps
